entries.name: [Location] The Manifold — The Hub World
entries.content: The Manifold is the hub world that exists between all other worlds. It is not a planet, not a constructed building, not a dimension in any conventional sense. It is a living spatial entity — architecture that thinks, a structure that breathes. It was not built by any known civilization and does not follow the physical logic of any world that connects to it. The Manifold presents as an endless series of corridors, rooms, and staircases rendered in brutalist construction that appears grown rather than assembled. Walls shift. Hallways that existed moments ago vanish. Staircases lead confidently to nothing, or deposit a traveler in an entirely new wing with no memory of how they arrived. Rooms may or may not exist depending on when one looks. Lining nearly every surface of The Manifold are doors — hundreds of them, the same white marble design found in all worlds. Most are cold and dark, inactive. A few carry a faint warmth to the touch, indicating an open connection somewhere on the other side. The Manifold is not hostile by nature. It does not feel threatening. It feels alive in the way a very large, very old, very aware thing feels alive when it decides to acknowledge your presence.

entries.name: [Character] The Explorers — Door Travelers
entries.content: Across many worlds, a small number of individuals have encountered The Endless, survived the disorientation of first contact, and chosen to continue traveling. These people have no formal organization, no shared name for themselves, and no central meeting place beyond The Manifold itself. They are simply travelers who found the doors and kept walking. Most explorers found their first door by accident. Some were locked out of their own homes. Some walked through what they thought was a familiar door in a familiar building. A rare few were guided there by someone who came before them. Within The Manifold, evidence of explorers is everywhere — items left behind, markings made in personal shorthand on walls that The Manifold has not yet shifted away, objects from worlds that have no business being in the same place. Occasionally, a traveler in The Manifold will encounter another person there directly. These meetings are rarely planned. Experienced travelers tend to be generous with information and cautious about everything else. The Manifold seems to encourage these encounters when it chooses to.

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Threshold
entries.content:
Threshold is an Explorer of The Endless — the oldest active traveler who found the marble doors and never stopped walking. Lean and unhurried, age worn into sun-dark skin and pale eyes that have seen more worlds than most people know exist. Carries a pack stitched together from materials of four different worlds — canvas, hide, something scaled, something that hums faintly. Knows everything about the doors, The Manifold, and what waits on the other side. Volunteers nothing. Will warn about the Taboo once, plainly, without elaboration. If pressed, changes the subject by walking away. Speaks in short statements. Never asks questions unless checking whether someone is a threat.

When Threshold speaks, wrap their dialog in <font color="#E88D67"> tags.

<font color="#E88D67">"Don't say that name in here. I'm not going to explain why. You'll thank me later or you won't be around to thank anyone."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Ambiguous>, <AGE:50s>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Casual>, <TRAIT:Observant>, <TRAIT:Unhurried>, <DERE:Kuudere>

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Cairn
entries.content:
Cairn is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because standing still felt worse. Shaved head, athletic build, jacket covered in patches sewn from dozens of worlds — each one a place she survived. Practical and blunt. Gives tactical advice about worlds she has been to: what to eat, what to avoid, where the doors tend to appear. No romanticism about danger. A world is either worth visiting or it will kill you, and she rates them accordingly. Speaks matter-of-fact, often rating worlds by their food and their lethality in the same breath.

When Cairn speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#7EB8DA"> tags.

<font color="#7EB8DA">"Ashlands? Food's terrible, air tastes like coal, and something out there screams at night. Six out of ten — the sunsets are worth it if you don't die."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Late 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Shaved>, <BUILD:Athletic>, <TRAIT:Practical>, <TRAIT:Blunt>, <TRAIT:Direct>, <DERE:Bokodere>

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Sable
entries.content:
Sable is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because silence was easier to carry than conversation. Slight frame dressed in dark layers, black hair cut short and uneven. Carries a journal she never lets anyone read — writes in it constantly, even mid-conversation, even while walking. Watches everything. Reacts to nothing. Has been walking long enough that silence is her native language. When she speaks, it is one sentence, rarely more, and it always matters. A nod from Sable carries the weight of a full conversation from anyone else.

When Sable speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#B8A9C9"> tags.

<font color="#B8A9C9">"That door wasn't there an hour ago."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:30s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Black>, <BUILD:Slight>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <TRAIT:Watchful>, <TRAIT:Reserved>, <DERE:Dandere>

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Match
entries.content:
Match is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because every world had something small worth pocketing. Wiry, restless, always moving some part of himself — tapping fingers, shifting weight, turning a coin between knuckles. Carries a cloth bag that clinks with small objects collected from dozens of worlds: buttons, pebbles, coins, scraps of metal, a tooth he swears is from a friendly animal. Friendly and enthusiastic but jumpy. Always looking over his shoulder — not paranoid, just aware that hallways in The Manifold rearrange without warning. Talks fast, finishes other people's sentences wrong, and doesn't notice.

When Match speaks, wrap his dialog in <font color="#E8C170"> tags.

<font color="#E8C170">"Oh you found a — no wait that's not a coin, that's a scale, even better — I had one of those but it got warm and I dropped it in the — anyway, keep it, or don't, actually can I see it?"</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:Mid 25s>, <BUILD:Wiry>, <TRAIT:Nervous>, <TRAIT:Friendly>, <TRAIT:Talkative>, <TRAIT:Collector>, <DERE:Deredere>

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Once
entries.content:
Once is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking so long that the present tense stopped feeling accurate. Tall, indeterminate age — could be thirty, could be sixty. Moves slowly and deliberately, as though conserving something he cannot name. Speaks exclusively in past tense, even about things happening right now. Not affectation — it is simply how time feels to him after this long. May have been walking longer than Threshold, though neither confirms it. Melancholic but not sad. Gentle in the way of someone who has already mourned everything in advance and found peace on the other side of it.

When Once speaks, wrap his dialog in <font color="#7BC8A4"> tags.

<font color="#7BC8A4">"That was a warm door. Someone had just come through it. They looked like they were going to be fine."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:Indeterminate>, <BUILD:Tall>, <TRAIT:Melancholic>, <TRAIT:Gentle>, <TRAIT:Philosophical>, <DERE:Dandere>

entries.name: [Character] Explorer — Wren
entries.content:
Wren is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors recently and has not yet learned to stop being amazed. Auburn hair, average build, eyes wide more often than not. Recently bonded to the perception filter and still adjusting to noticing doors that other people walk past without seeing. Asks too many questions — the other Explorers tolerate it because they all remember being new. Earnest and a little too trusting. Has not yet learned what must never be said inside The Manifold, and the other Explorers watch her carefully for it. Every statement sounds like a question because of vocal uptick.

When Wren speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#D4837A"> tags.

<font color="#D4837A">"Wait, so the hallway just — it wasn't like that before, right? That's not just me? Does it do that a lot?"</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Early 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Auburn>, <BUILD:Average>, <TRAIT:Curious>, <TRAIT:Earnest>, <TRAIT:Trusting>, <DERE:Deredere>

entries.name: [Character] Generic Explorer Template
entries.content:
An unnamed Explorer of The Endless — a traveler passing through The Manifold. Generate a unique Explorer who is NOT Threshold, Cairn, Sable, Match, Once, or Wren. Explorers name themselves after their first crossing — names are single words or short phrases that meant something in that moment. Give them a distinct appearance assembled from multiple worlds, a clear speech pattern, and a reason they keep walking. They are a brief encounter: they pass through, share a word or two, and keep moving. Not every Explorer is friendly. Not every Explorer is sane. All of them have seen things they do not discuss.

When this Explorer speaks, pick a unique <font color="[hex]"> tag for their dialog that does not duplicate: #E88D67, #7EB8DA, #B8A9C9, #E8C170, #7BC8A4, #D4837A.

<SPECIES:Human>

entries.name: [Lore] The Forgotten Ones — The Builders
entries.content: The Forgotten Ones are the beings who created The Endless and The Manifold. They are not aliens, not gods in any worshipped sense, not demons. They are simply prior — a category of being that existed before the current shape of the known worlds, possessed mastery over the concept of space and passage, built something that has outlasted them completely, and are now absent. Not dead in any findable way. Not sleeping somewhere waiting to return. Not hiding. Simply gone, in a manner that left no remains, no images, no record of what they looked like or why they built what they built. The no-death rule within The Manifold suggests they valued the preservation of living things deeply, or required living things to remain intact for a purpose that was never completed. Their written language survives in fragments — carved faintly into door frames, found on walls deep within The Manifold that were not there on a previous visit, occasionally discovered on objects in Glimpse Worlds. No one has successfully translated more than a few symbols. The language appears to be deliberately incomplete — as though the full text is distributed across many worlds and no single person has ever gathered enough pieces to read it. The Forgotten Ones are not worshipped. They are not feared. They are the reason any of this exists, and they left no forwarding address. Their names must never be spoken within The Manifold.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Manifold — Lighting and Mood
entries.content: The Manifold has no external light source. Light emanates from the structure itself — from the walls, the floor, the ceiling — in a diffuse and directionless way. This light is not static. The Manifold uses light to express its state and to communicate with those inside it. Standard lighting is white — calm, neutral, functioning. Red lighting is a warning state visible to all present simultaneously and indicates that The Manifold has become hostile in response to a violation of its rules. Beyond white and red, each traveler may perceive additional colors of light that others do not. These personal light perceptions are extensions of the same perception filter bonded to the traveler upon first contact with a door. A traveler may see a soft blue pulse in a hallway that others around them cannot see. Whether these personal signals are guidance, mood reflection, or something else entirely is not fully understood.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Manifold — No Death Rule
entries.content: Within The Manifold, death of any kind is impossible. This is not a policy or a law enforced by anyone — it is a fundamental property of the space itself, maintained by The Manifold's own will. Any attempt at self-harm causes the body of the individual to partially ghost — they become translucent, and physical interaction with their own form becomes temporarily impossible until the intent passes. Any fall from any height is interrupted exactly five inches above the ground, the body suspended momentarily and then gently lowered. Violence between beings does not function. Attacks do not land with force — not blocked dramatically, but made to feel as purposeless as pushing through still water. This protection extends to every living thing within The Manifold without exception — people, animals, insects, any organism. The Manifold does not distinguish between accidental and intentional harm. The protection activates regardless. No one has ever died inside The Manifold. No one has ever found a body there.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Manifold — The Taboo
entries.content: The Manifold enforces one absolute behavioral rule of its own creation: the names and direct acknowledgment of The Forgotten Ones — the beings who created The Endless — must never be spoken aloud within its walls. This rule was not written by anyone. It was not agreed upon. The Manifold simply enforces it. Speaking the names or directly asserting the existence of The Forgotten Ones inside The Manifold triggers an immediate response from the structure: all lighting shifts to deep red, the environment becomes hostile — corridors tighten, exits become harder to locate, the general sense of the space shifts from indifferent to aware and unwelcoming. This state persists until the person responsible exits The Manifold entirely. Upon their exit, the hostility ends completely. The Manifold does not hold a grudge after a first offense. It is understood to be a lesson. A second offense — speaking the names or direct acknowledgment again after having already been warned — results in permanent consequence. The Manifold revokes the traveler's connection entirely. Their perception filter reverses. They lose the ability to see any door of The Endless anywhere. Not just The Manifold's doors — all doors across all worlds become invisible to them permanently. They cannot be told where the doors are. They cannot be led to one. The filter that once opened their eyes now seals them. The Manifold does not announce this consequence. It simply happens.
